title-group.article-title: How to Design and Implement a Structured Curriculum in Health Professions Education: A Nine‐Step Framework Building on Kern's Approach

aff.named-content: University Department of General Practice, Faculty of Health

aff.institution: Maison de Santé Pluriprofessionnelle Universitaire « La Providence »

aff.named-content: Center for Epidemiology and Research in Population Health (CERPOP)
aff.institution: University of Toulouse, INSERM UMR1295, UPS

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). The Clinical Teacher published by Association for the Study of Medical Education and John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Structured approaches to curriculum development help improve coherence between learning objectives, teaching strategies and assessment in health professions education [1, 2]. Kern's six‐step model is widely used to guide curriculum design and implementation [3]. However, implementation, sustainability, faculty engagement and dissemination are not always explicitly addressed in curricular projects [4, 5]. Building on Kern's framework allowed integration of these complementary dimensions while maintaining a familiar structure for educators. This article proposes a practical nine‐step framework informed by the medical education literature, including perspectives related to educational scholarship and dissemination [6].

disp-quote.p: Building on Kern's framework allowed integration of these complementary dimensions while maintaining a familiar structure for educators.

sec.title: Theoretical Foundations
sec.p: Kern's six‐step approach served as the core structural framework. The six steps are: (1) problem identification and general needs assessment, (2) targeted needs assessment, (3) goals and objectives, (4) educational strategies, (5) implementation and (6) evaluation and feedback [2].

sec.title: Narrative Literature Review
sec.p: We conducted a narrative literature review inspired by the principles of theoretical integrative reviews, with the aim of critically synthesizing and connecting existing theoretical and empirical work related to our topic [7]. The aim was to identify conceptual and methodological elements complementing Kern's framework, particularly regarding implementation, sustainability and scholarship.

Searches were conducted in PubMed and ERIC using combinations of keywords such as ‘curriculum development’, ‘educational innovation’, ‘implementation’ and ‘faculty development’. Grey literature (institutional reports, accreditation standards and faculty development resources) was also searched. Selection prioritized conceptual papers, reviews and guidance documents. Findings were analysed using thematic synthesis to identify recurring concepts not operationalized in Kern's model [2, 7].

sec.title: Description of the Nine‐Steps
sec.p: We identified three recurring domains that were insufficiently operationalized in Kern's framework: (1) explicit use of conceptual/theoretical frameworks, (2) long‐term sustainability and (3) scholarly dissemination. These domains directly informed the addition of Steps 3, 8 and 9 in the proposed model. The identification of implementation, sustainability and scholarly dissemination as key areas for extension was based on their recurrent presence in the literature and their practical relevance in faculty development contexts. These domains were therefore conceptualized as critical components required to move from curriculum design to sustainable curricular implementation.
The nine steps listed below are represented in Figure 1.
Table 1 identifies which steps are derived from Kern's model and which steps are newly introduced.

caption.p: Mapping of the nine‐step framework to Kern's six‐step model.

tr.td: Steps 1 and 2
Steps 1–3
Equivalent (needs and objectives)
Step 3
New (theoretical grounding)
Step 4
Step 4
Equivalent (educational strategies)
Steps 5 and 6
Step 5
Expanded (planning + implementation)
Step 7
Step 6
Equivalent (evaluation)
Steps 8 and 9
New (sustainability + scholarship)

sec.p: To enhance the practical applicability of the framework, we illustrated each of the nine steps with specific examples derived from a general practice educational project. These detailed examples are provided in Appendix 1 at the end of the manuscript.

sec.title: Step 1: Needs Assessment
sec.p: The first step consists of identifying a clearly defined educational problem and the associated learning needs. Following Kern et al., this analysis aims to determine the gap between current and desired performance [2]. Data may be collected from multiple sources, including learner feedback, assessment results, observations of clinical practice, accreditation standards and institutional priorities. Attention is paid to contextual determinants such as organizational culture, available resources and curricular constraints. This step provides an empirical and contextual justification for the curricular project and forms the foundation of the entire project [1].

sec.title: Step 2: Definition of Learning Objectives
sec.p: Based on the needs assessment, educators define clear, specific and measurable learning objectives [8]. These objectives specify the competencies to be developed, including knowledge, skills and professional attitudes or values. Best practices recommend alignment with competency frameworks and the use of observable action verbs, informed by Bloom's revised taxonomy and Miller's pyramid [9, 10]. Well‐formulated objectives ensure clarity of intent and coherence between teaching, learning and assessment strategies.

sec.title: Step 3: Selection of a Conceptual and Theoretical Framework
sec.p: Curricular projects should be grounded in explicit conceptual and learning theories that justify pedagogical choices [2]. Depending on the nature of the objectives and context, educators may draw on various theoretical perspectives, including, for example, constructivist and socioconstructivist theories, experiential learning, transformative learning, situated learning or cognitive load theory. Making the theoretical framework explicit enhances pedagogical coherence, supports design decisions (e.g., choice of methods, sequencing and level of guidance) and strengthens the academic legitimacy and transferability of the educational project [4].

sec.title: Step 4: Design of Educational Strategies
sec.p: In this step, learning objectives are translated into concrete educational activities and teaching methods. Principles of instructional design and constructive alignment guide the selection of strategies such as problem‐ or case‐based learning, guided clinical reasoning workshops, simulation with structured debriefing, deliberate practice or feedback‐rich supervision models [11]. Supporting materials, assessment tools and feedback mechanisms are developed to create an integrated, learner‐centred educational intervention [12]. This step also includes consideration of the learning environment (e.g., clinical setting, institutional culture and interprofessional context), which may influence the feasibility and effectiveness of educational strategies.

sec.title: Step 5: Planning
sec.p: Planning transforms the educational design into an operational project. This step includes defining the sequence and timing of activities, establishing a realistic implementation timeline, allocating human and material resources, clarifying roles and responsibilities and identifying process and outcome indicators of success [2]. Effective planning anticipates potential barriers (e.g., workload, scheduling and resistance to change) and facilitates coordination among stakeholders, enabling a smooth transition from design to implementation [13]. Consideration of sustainability should begin at this stage, including resource planning, institutional alignment and long‐term feasibility.

sec.title: Step 6: Implementation
sec.p: Implementation corresponds to the launch of the educational project in real conditions [14]. It often involves a pilot phase, the orientation or training of teachers and clinical supervisors and active support for change. Transparent communication about objectives, expected benefits and evaluation plans is essential to foster engagement and alignment. Early feedback from learners and faculty is intentionally collected (e.g., rapid cycle evaluation and debriefings) to allow timely adaptation and support adoption of the curricular intervention [3]. Implementation should therefore be viewed as an adaptive process informed by ongoing feedback and iterative adjustments.

sec.title: Step 7: Evaluation
sec.p: Evaluation is an integral and continuous component of the educational project rather than a final, isolated step. Multiple dimensions may be assessed, including learner satisfaction, achievement of learning objectives, changes in attitudes or behaviours, the transfer of learning to clinical practice and broader organizational or patient‐related impact [15]. Established evaluation frameworks such as Kirkpatrick's model, combined with assessment frameworks such as Miller's pyramid, guide the selection of appropriate assessment methods [10, 16]. Evaluation findings inform iterative improvement, support accountability and provide evidence of effectiveness for scholarly dissemination and institutional decision making [2, 7].

sec.title: Step 8: Sustainability
sec.p: While sustainability is presented here as a distinct step to emphasize its importance, it should be anticipated throughout the design and planning phases of the project.

Sustainability addresses the long‐term integration of the curricular intervention within the curriculum and institutional culture [17]. This step includes embedding the programme into official curricula, aligning it with accreditation or institutional priorities, securing leadership and administrative support and ensuring the availability and renewal of human and material resources. Sustainability involves the establishment of continuous feedback and quality improvement mechanisms, ensuring that the curricular intervention evolves in response to changing contexts and needs. It also involves fostering communities of practice or faculty networks and establishing processes for regular review and adaptation [3]. Planned attention to sustainability increases the continuity, reproducibility and resilience of the curricular project over time, beyond individual champions.

sec.title: Step 9: Valorization and Dissemination
sec.p: The final step focuses on sharing and recognizing the value of the curricular project. This step is closely aligned with the Scholarship of Teaching and Learning (SoTL), emphasizing systematic inquiry, peer‐reviewed dissemination and the contribution to collective educational knowledge [6].

Dissemination may occur through publications, conference presentations, institutional seminars, faculty development activities and professional networks [4]. Valorization emphasizes reflective practice, formal documentation of processes and outcomes and collective learning within and across institutions. By conceptualizing valorization and dissemination as integral components of the methodological framework, this step contributes to the advancement of health professions education and encourages transfer and adaptation of effective practices across contexts.
For clarity, Table 2 provides a structured overview of the process, detailing the main purpose, expected outcomes, key references and relevant comments or contributions for each step.

caption.p: Nine‐step methodological framework for planning and implementing an educational innovation project in health professions education.

tr.td: 1. Needs assessment
Identify the educational problems, learner expectations and institutional context.
Comprehensive diagnosis of the educational need (gaps between current and desired situation, identified constraints and resources).
Kern et al. (2016); Prideaux (2003); Pelaccia (2016)
First engineering step: understanding the context and justifying the innovation. Provides an empirical foundation for the project.
2. Definition of objectives
Determine the competencies to be developed and formulate measurable objectives.
Learning objectives formulated using taxonomies (Bloom, Miller) and aligned with competency frameworks and training standards.
Anderson and Krathwohl (2001); Tardif (2013); Harden (2007)
Ensures clarity of educational goals and coherence with the targeted competency profile.
3. Choice of conceptual framework
Provide a coherent theoretical basis for the learning approach.
Explicit choice of one or more learning models (experiential, constructivist, transformative, etc.) that justify the instructional design.
Kolb (1984); Vygotsky (1978); Mezirow (1991); Lave and Wenger (1991); Sweller (1988); Taylor and Hamdy (2013)
Anchors the innovation in a recognized scientific framework, strengthening its academic legitimacy.
4. Design of educational strategies
Develop learning activities, tools and teaching methods.
Detailed plan of activities aligned with the objectives: scenarios, learning resources, assessment tools and feedback mechanisms.
Biggs and Tang (2011); Harden (2001); Pelaccia (2016); Audétat and Laurin (2010)
Instructional design step: creation of an integrated, active learning intervention.
5. Planning
Organize the concrete implementation: sequencing, schedule, resources, actors and indicators.
Validated operational plan (timeline, roles, resources, success indicators, communication plan).
Kern et al. (2016); Harden (2009); Tardif and Meirieu (2010)
Structuring step: moving from the concept to a concrete project.
6. Implementation
Launch the programme, train stakeholders and support change.
Pilot or initial implementation conducted; faculty trained; first feedback from participants collected.
Cantillon and Wood (2010); Pelaccia (2016); Irby (1994)
Key moment of change: learning moves from planning to practice.
7. Evaluation
Measure the effects on learning, practice and the educational system.
Quantitative and qualitative data collected at different levels (reaction, learning, behaviour, outcomes).
Kirkpatrick and Kirkpatrick (2006); Miller (1990); Nendaz and Charlin (2005)
‘Evaluate to improve’: allows adjustment of the intervention and provides evidence of effectiveness.
8. Sustainability
Integrate the innovation durably into the curriculum and institutional culture.
Stabilized, integrated and supported intervention; continuous quality improvement processes in place.
Harden (2009); Biggs and Tang (2011); Tardif (2013)
Ensures continuity, reproducibility and institutional anchoring of the innovation.
9. Valorization
Disseminate, share and have the value of the innovation recognized
Publications, conference presentations, institutional recognition and creation of communities of practice.
Schön (1983); Mezirow (1991); Steinert et al. (2016); Audétat and Caire Fon (2018)
Highlights the reflective and collective dimension of the innovation; encourages sharing and transfer of practices.

fn.p: References reported in the “Key references” column are provided for explanatory purposes only and are not cited in the main text or included in the reference list.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The integration of theoretical grounding may support greater coherence between learning objectives, educational strategies, implementation planning and evaluation processes. Similarly, explicit consideration of sustainability and dissemination may facilitate longer‐term integration of curricular projects within institutional environments and contribute to scholarly dissemination and shared curricular knowledge [17].

The framework also emphasizes the importance of iterative evaluation and adaptation during implementation. This perspective is consistent with contemporary approaches in implementation science and quality improvement, which highlight the value of continuous feedback and contextual responsiveness in curriculum implementation [18, 19].

disp-quote.p: The framework also emphasizes the importance of iterative evaluation and adaptation during implementation.

sec.p: Several limitations should be acknowledged. The framework was informed by a narrative rather than a systematic review and has not yet been formally evaluated across different health professions education contexts. In addition, its transferability across different educational contexts remains hypothetical and requires further empirical exploration.

Future research should examine the usability, perceived utility and applicability of the framework across diverse curricular and institutional contexts, as well as its potential impact on learner and programme outcomes [20].

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This nine‐step framework builds on Kern's model to provide a practical and structured approach to the design, implementation, evaluation and long‐term integration of curricular projects in health professions education.

disp-quote.p: This nine‐step framework builds on Kern's model to provide a practical and structured approach to the design, implementation, evaluation and long‐term integration of curricular projects in health professions education.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Émile Escourrou: conceptualization, investigation, writing – original draft, methodology, formal analysis, writing – review and editing, validation, resources. Stéphane Oustric: visualization, supervision.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The authors have nothing to report.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: Ethical approval was not required for this methodological article, as it does not involve human participants or identifiable data.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The author thanks Anne Demeester who initiated this reflection. Open access publication funding provided by COUPERIN CY26.
The author used the Perplexity AI writing assistant and ChatGPT to assist with the reformulation and language correction.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author. The data are not publicly available due to privacy or ethical restrictions.

ref.mixed-citation: D. Prideaux , “Curriculum Development in Medical Education: From Theory to Practice,” Australian and New Zealand Journal of Surgery 73, no. 5 (2003): 367–370.

P. Nilsen , “Making Sense of Implementation Theories, Models and Frameworks,” Implementation Science 10 (2015): 53.25895742 10.1186/s13012-015-0242-0PMC4406164

F. M. Chen , H. Bauchner , and H. Burstin , “A Call for Outcomes Research in Medical Education,” Journal of the American Medical Association 292, no. 9 (2004): 1057–1060.15383351 10.1097/00001888-200410000-00010

app.label: Appendix 1
app.title: Illustrative Example of Application of the Nine‐Step Framework in General Practice Training

sec.title: Step 1: Needs Assessment
sec.p: In a general practice training programme, supervisors report that trainees experience difficulties in clinical reasoning when managing patients with undifferentiated symptoms, such as fatigue or abdominal pain. Chart reviews and direct observation reveal challenges in prioritizing hypotheses and avoiding premature diagnostic closure. These issues are particularly evident in contexts of multimorbidity and time‐constrained consultations. Improving clinical reasoning in uncertainty is therefore identified as a priority educational need.

sec.title: Step 2: Definition of Learning Objectives
sec.p: Based on the identified needs, learning objectives are defined to strengthen clinical reasoning in primary care. For example, trainees are expected to generate and prioritize differential diagnoses in situations of diagnostic uncertainty, explicitly articulate their reasoning and revise their hypotheses as new information becomes available. These objectives are aligned with competency frameworks in general practice and formulated using observable action verbs.

sec.title: Step 3: Selection of a Conceptual and Theoretical Framework
sec.p: The educational design is informed by dual‐process theory and illness script theory. Dual‐process theory helps address the balance between intuitive and analytical reasoning, while illness script theory supports the organization of clinical knowledge in primary care contexts. These frameworks guide the design of learning activities that promote both pattern recognition and reflective reasoning, ensuring coherence between objectives, teaching strategies and assessment methods.

sec.title: Step 4: Design of Educational Strategies
sec.p: The educational strategy includes case‐based discussions using authentic primary care scenarios, reflective practice sessions and structured supervision focusing on reasoning processes. Clinical cases are selected to reflect common presentations with diagnostic uncertainty, such as nonspecific symptoms or early‐stage diseases. The learning environment—characterized by continuity of care, limited consultation time and patient complexity—is explicitly considered. Tools such as reflective writing prompts and structured feedback guides are integrated to support learning.

sec.title: Step 5: Planning
sec.p: The programme is integrated into existing general practice rotations, with scheduled case discussion sessions and supervisor‐led reflective debriefings. Faculty development sessions are organized to train supervisors in facilitating discussions on clinical reasoning. Resources such as case libraries and feedback tools are developed. Potential barriers, including time constraints in busy clinics, are anticipated, and strategies such as brief, structured discussions are planned. Sustainability considerations are addressed by aligning the programme with curriculum requirements.

sec.title: Step 6: Implementation
sec.p: The programme is initially implemented with a small group of trainees and volunteer supervisors. Early sessions focus on introducing structured case discussions and encouraging explicit verbalization of reasoning. Feedback from participants leads to adjustments, such as simplifying discussion frameworks and integrating activities into routine clinical supervision to minimize additional workload.

sec.title: Step 7: Evaluation
sec.p: Evaluation includes trainee self‐assessment of confidence in clinical reasoning, supervisor assessments during consultations and analysis of case presentations. Qualitative data from reflective writings and feedback sessions provide insight into changes in reasoning processes. These data are used to refine the educational intervention and improve its alignment with learners' needs.

sec.title: Step 8: Sustainability
sec.p: To ensure sustainability, the programme is embedded within the general practice curriculum and supported by training coordinators. A group of trained supervisors is established to maintain the initiative. Regular review meetings are conducted to update case materials and adapt the programme to evolving clinical and educational contexts, ensuring its continued relevance.

sec.title: Step 9: Valorization and Dissemination
sec.p: The project team shares the results through presentations at primary care education conferences and submits a manuscript describing the intervention and its outcomes. Locally, the programme is presented during faculty development workshops, encouraging adoption by other training sites and contributing to a broader community of practice in general practice education.
